summary:
Graft placement is most likely to fail in Classes III or IV recessions due to their severe nature compromising underlying support structures necessary for successful healing post-surgery. The variability observed across various surgical techniques underscores that while some may succeed even under challenging conditions like Class II receding gums, significant difficulties persist when dealing directly with advanced stages such as those classified at levels three through four where substantial tissue loss has occurred already making it harder to achieve predictable outcomes despite best efforts made during intervention attempts involving flap surgery combined with autograft materials etc... To reiterate emphasis placed earlier regarding complexity inherent within higher grade lesions necessitating careful consideration before proceeding towards any formative corrective action aimed specifically targeting them without thorough assessment first being conducted thoroughly beforehand including patient history review along side imaging studies whenever possible so informed decisions can then guide appropriate treatment planning moving forward accordingly...
To summarize concisely based strictly upon provided instructions now only retaining essential information needed per requirement stated above :

Graft placement tends to have lower success rate particularly noted amongst patients suffering from severe (Class III/IV) periodontal defects characterized primarily via extensive soft-tissue losses thereby increasing procedural complexities significantly compared against milder forms seen typically associated lesser degrees overall damage sustained thus far allowing better prognosis generally speaking...
